metadata.title: Proposed Additions to the Housing Action Plan
metadata.status: Proposed — for Housing Committee and Planning Department review
metadata.source: External expert input, Tom Morgan, AICP (April 30, 2026)
metadata.intake_protocol: Tom Morgan, AICP — a credentialed planner in another NH community — sent Progress Portsmouth a thoughtful set of comments on the Housing Action Plan. Some of his suggestions translate cleanly into action items or refinements; others are good points that don't fit the plan format. The four below are the ones we thought were worth your eyes. Two are new items, two are proposed enrichments to existing items. None of them have been merged — that's your call.
review_questions: Want to add it as drafted?
If yes, what canonical ID should it get?

proposed_items.action: Resident Owned Communities (ROC) preservation and expansion strategy
proposed_items.description: Phase 1: Inventory manufactured housing parks and resident-owned cooperatives in and adjacent to Portsmouth, working with the NH Community Loan Fund. Phase 2: Adopt zoning provisions that recognize manufactured housing on owned land as a permitted residential type in appropriate zones. Phase 3: Establish a city policy supporting resident purchase of manufactured housing parks when owners list them for sale, including a right-of-first-refusal mechanism modeled on NH RSA 205-A or equivalent local authorization. Phase 4: Coordinate with the Housing Trust Fund to provide gap financing for resident cooperative formation when ROC-USA or NH Community Loan Fund underwriting falls short.
proposed_items.target: Preserve all existing manufactured housing units in and adjacent to Portsmouth as permanently affordable through resident ownership. Identify potential conversion sites within the Seacoast region.
proposed_items.metric: Number of ROC units preserved or created; number of manufactured housing parks converted to resident ownership; capital deployed through Housing Trust Fund gap financing for ROC formation.
proposed_items.sources: Tom Morgan, AICP (April 30, 2026); NH Community Loan Fund — list of NH Resident Owned Communities (https://communityloanfund.org/resources/list-of-nh-resident-owned-communities); NH RSA 205-A; Seabrook ROC precedent (292 units across multiple cooperatives); Woodbury Cooperative
proposed_items.notes: ROCs are a distinct production and preservation pathway — cooperative member equity plus ROC-USA financing, with their own legal vehicle and policy levers. Tom Morgan offered to facilitate a tour of the Woodbury Cooperative for the Housing Committee; Seabrook's ~292 owner-occupied ROC units are the closest large-scale Seacoast precedent. NH Community Loan Fund handles the technical assistance and financing intermediation. Worth checking whether any manufactured housing parcels in or near Portsmouth face conversion pressure that a ROC strategy could intercept.
pros: Preserves existing affordable units that are at risk of conversion to market-rate or non-residential uses when parks are sold.
Creates a permanent affordability mechanism through cooperative ownership rather than time-limited subsidy.
Builds household wealth for moderate-income owners through equity in the cooperative.
Activates a production pathway that does not require new construction or land acquisition by the city.
cons: Portsmouth's manufactured housing inventory is limited; the strategy may yield few in-city units and require regional coordination.
Right-of-first-refusal mechanisms require enabling state legislation or careful local authorization and may face owner opposition.
ROC formation is staff- and capital-intensive; success depends on willing cooperatives and financing alignment.

proposed_items.action: Federal Historic Preservation Tax Credit utilization strategy
proposed_items.description: Operationalizes I-18 (Historic preservation + housing incentives) by naming the Federal Historic Preservation Tax Credit as the principal capital tool. Phase 1: Identify all Portsmouth properties currently listed on the National Register of Historic Places or eligible for listing that have housing redevelopment potential. Phase 2: Establish a city protocol for supporting Federal Historic Preservation Tax Credit applications, including pre-application consultation, alignment of local historic district review with National Park Service Standards for Rehabilitation, and coordination with the NH Division of Historical Resources. Phase 3: Pair the federal credit with local tools (RSA 79-E where applicable, Housing Trust Fund gap financing, density bonuses for retained historic structures) to make adaptive reuse projects pencil. Phase 4: Document and learn from the Franklin Block experience — what city processes, applicant communications, or staff capacity gaps caused that project to falter, and what changes would prevent recurrence.
proposed_items.target: Activate the Federal Historic Preservation Tax Credit as a primary capital tool for adaptive reuse housing projects on eligible Portsmouth properties.
proposed_items.metric: Number of Portsmouth historic preservation tax credit applications submitted; number approved; housing units produced through federally-credited rehab; total federal credit value captured.
proposed_items.sources: Tom Morgan, AICP (April 30, 2026); National Park Service Federal Historic Preservation Tax Incentives program; NH Division of Historical Resources; precedents — Newmarket mill rehabilitations, Saco mill conversions, Hilltop School (Somersworth); Portsmouth Franklin Block (cautionary precedent)
proposed_items.notes: The 20% federal credit on qualified rehab expenses is a substantial capital source for adaptive reuse on income-producing National Register properties. NH and ME both have multiple successful precedents at comparable scale. Portsmouth has tried it once (Franklin Block) and ran into trouble; documenting the cause matters before assuming the tool itself is at fault. Drafted as an amendment to I-18 rather than a standalone item because I-18 already names the category — this just makes the principal federal tool explicit and operational.
pros: Activates a substantial federal capital source (20% credit on qualified rehab expenses) without requiring new local appropriations.
Strong regional precedents (Newmarket, Saco, Somersworth) demonstrate feasibility for buildings of comparable scale and condition to Portsmouth's historic stock.
Pairs cleanly with state RSA 79-E and local Housing Trust Fund gap financing, creating a stackable capital structure.
Encourages adaptive reuse over demolition, addressing Portsmouth's historic preservation and housing production goals simultaneously.
cons: Requires applicant-side technical capacity (historic tax credit specialists, qualified rehabilitation accounting) that not all Portsmouth property owners have.
National Park Service Standards for Rehabilitation can constrain design flexibility, particularly for affordability-driven projects with thin margins.
City staff capacity for pre-application consultation and review coordination is a limiting factor; the Franklin Block precedent suggests this gap is real.

proposed_items.action: Cottages at Dover precedent and reference
proposed_items.description: A reference and notes addition to Z-9 (Cottage housing / cluster development provisions). No new zoning text. Adds the Cottages at Dover, NH project as a documented NH precedent for cottage cluster facilitation, and identifies project principals Maggie and John Randolph as available technical references. The Dover facilitation model reportedly involved active municipal engagement with developer, designer, and prospective residents during entitlement; documenting what Dover did would inform Portsmouth's Z-9 implementation as it expands from the GNOD to other zones.
proposed_items.target: Document the Cottages at Dover facilitation model and apply transferable elements to Portsmouth's Z-9 implementation.
proposed_items.metric: Documentation of Dover facilitation steps; Z-9 implementation guidance updated to reflect transferable practices; number of cottage cluster applications received in Portsmouth.
proposed_items.sources: Tom Morgan, AICP (April 30, 2026); Cottages at Dover, NH project (Maggie and John Randolph, principals); City of Dover Planning Department
proposed_items.notes: Not a standalone HAP item — a notes addition to Z-9. Tom Morgan offered to facilitate introductions to the Randolphs. Dover's process is the closest NH precedent for cottage cluster facilitation and is worth understanding before Z-9 expansion proceeds. Question for Planning staff: has the Department already been in contact with Dover Planning on their cottage cluster experience? If so, this becomes a confirmation rather than a new lead.
pros: Provides a documented NH precedent for the Z-9 implementation team to reference.
Identifies named technical resources (the Randolphs, Dover Planning staff) available for direct consultation.
Adds context to Z-9 without creating a new line-item or competing with existing language.
cons: Dover's market dynamics and parcel patterns differ from Portsmouth's; not all facilitation elements will transfer.
If the Cottages at Dover experience involved significant municipal subsidies or staff time, the Portsmouth equivalent may exceed what staff capacity allows.

proposed_items.action: Floodplain overlay zone map accuracy review and refresh
proposed_items.description: Phase 1: Compare the maps used in Portsmouth's 2019 floodplain overlay zone adoption with the current FEMA Flood Insurance Rate Maps (FIRMs) and any subsequent updates. Phase 2: Identify parcels where the overlay restricts buildable area based on map data that may be outdated, inaccurate, or superseded. Phase 3: Where map accuracy issues are found, initiate a process to update the overlay using current FEMA data, including public notification of affected property owners in advance of any further amendment, and incorporation of any newer flood modeling (sea-level rise projections, current Piscataqua River dynamics). Phase 4: If the original 2019 adoption is found to have notification or process deficiencies, document those for the public record and inform any future overlay amendments accordingly.
proposed_items.target: Ensure the floodplain overlay zone reflects current and accurate flood hazard data, with adequate property owner notification on any amendment.
proposed_items.metric: Number of parcels where overlay-restricted area changes following review; documentation of map source comparison; record of property owner notification process for any amendment.
proposed_items.sources: Tom Morgan, AICP (April 30, 2026); Portsmouth Zoning Ordinance, Floodplain Overlay District; FEMA Flood Insurance Rate Maps; NH Coastal Adaptation Workgroup
proposed_items.notes: External input (Tom Morgan) raised concerns that the 2019 floodplain overlay was adopted without accurate maps and that property owners were notified only after adoption. These specific characterizations should be verified with City Clerk records and Planning Department files before the item proceeds. Independent of Tom's specific characterization, the underlying technical question — whether the 2019 overlay reflects current FEMA data and whether the maps in use accurately depict current and projected flood hazards — is a legitimate one with implications for buildable area citywide and for the city's coastal resilience planning. Best framed as a technical map review with public-process implications, not as a critique of the 2019 adoption.
pros: Aligns regulatory restrictions with current and accurate flood hazard data, supporting both housing production on incorrectly restricted parcels and appropriate restriction on parcels where flood risk has increased.
Addresses a procedural concern raised in public input regarding the 2019 adoption notification process.
Creates a documented refresh cadence for the overlay, supporting the city's broader coastal resilience and Climate Action Plan goals.
Integrates with CS-3 (Flood resilience and climate adaptation) and the CRIZ 79-E coastal resilience incentive zone work.
cons: Reopening the 2019 overlay invites political contestation from property owners with stakes in either direction.
Map review is technical work that may exceed Planning Department staff capacity without consultant support.
If the review confirms the 2019 maps were accurate, the item produces no buildable-area gains; if it finds inaccuracies, the amendment process is itself substantial.
